answer:
Definition

An interviewer-grade deep dive into Input Validation examines validation at every system boundary: request body/query/params validation (schema-based), business rule validation (domain logic), cross-field validation, file upload validation, SQL/NoSQL injection prevention, and the trade-offs between client-side and server-side validation. Failure modes include validation bypass via API directly, inconsistent validation between client and server, and performance degradation from overly complex schemas.

Concrete Use-Case

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj implemented multi-layer validation for the leave creation endpoint: (1) Zod schema validates request shape (dates are ISO strings, type is enum, days > 0), (2) Business rules: no weekends, no backdating, no overlap with existing leaves, balance check, (3) Cross-field: endDate > startDate, days matches actual business days between dates, (4) Authorization: employee can only submit for themselves, manager can submit on behalf.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

import { z } from 'zod';
import { differenceInBusinessDays, isWeekend, isBefore } from 'date-fns';

// Layer 1: Schema validation with Zod
const CreateLeaveSchema = z.object({
  startDate: z.string().datetime().refine(d => !isBefore(new Date(d), new Date()), {
    message: 'Cannot backdate leave requests',
  }),
  endDate: z.string().datetime(),
  type: z.enum(['ANNUAL', 'SICK', 'PERSONAL', 'MATERNITY']),
  reason: z.string().min(10).max(500).optional(),
}).refine(data => new Date(data.endDate) > new Date(data.startDate), {
  message: 'End date must be after start date',
  path: ['endDate'],
}).refine(data => {
  const days = differenceInBusinessDays(new Date(data.endDate), new Date(data.startDate));
  return days > 0 && days <= 30;
}, { message: 'Leave duration must be 1-30 business days' });

// Layer 2: Business rule validation
async function validateBusinessRules(data: CreateLeaveInput, userId: string) {
  const errors: string[] = [];

  // No weekend start/end
  if (isWeekend(new Date(data.startDate))) errors.push('Leave cannot start on a weekend');
  if (isWeekend(new Date(data.endDate))) errors.push('Leave cannot end on a weekend');

  // Overlap check
  const overlapping = await prisma.leave.findFirst({
    where: {
      employeeId: userId,
      status: { in: ['PENDING', 'APPROVED'] },
      OR: [
        { startDate: { lte: new Date(data.endDate) }, endDate: { gte: new Date(data.startDate) } },
      ],
    },
  });
  if (overlapping) errors.push('Overlaps with existing leave request');

  // Balance check
  const balance = await prisma.leaveBalance.findUnique({ where: { employeeId: userId } });
  const days = differenceInBusinessDays(new Date(data.endDate), new Date(data.startDate));
  if (balance && balance.remaining < days) {
    errors.push(`Insufficient balance: ${balance.remaining} days remaining, ${days} requested`);
  }

  return errors;
}

// Layer 3: Security sanitization
function sanitizeInput(data: any): any {
  if (typeof data === 'string') {
    // Remove potential NoSQL injection operators
    return data.replace(/[${}]/g, '');
  }
  if (Array.isArray(data)) return data.map(sanitizeInput);
  if (typeof data === 'object' && data !== null) {
    return Object.fromEntries(
      Object.entries(data).map(([k, v]) => [k, sanitizeInput(v)])
    );
  }
  return data;
}

// Combined validation middleware
app.post('/api/leaves', async (req, reply) => {
  // Schema validation
  const parsed = CreateLeaveSchema.safeParse(req.body);
  if (!parsed.success) {
    return reply.code(400).send({
      error: 'Validation failed',
      details: parsed.error.issues.map(i => ({
        field: i.path.join('.'),
        message: i.message,
      })),
    });
  }

  // Business validation
  const businessErrors = await validateBusinessRules(parsed.data, req.user.id);
  if (businessErrors.length > 0) {
    return reply.code(422).send({ error: 'Business rule violation', details: businessErrors });
  }

  // Create leave
  const leave = await prisma.leave.create({ data: { ...parsed.data, employeeId: req.user.id } });
  reply.code(201).send(leave);
});

Debugging Narrative

Situation: An employee discovered they could submit a leave request starting yesterday by modifying the Angular form's date picker value via browser DevTools. The server accepted it because validation only checked if the date was a valid ISO string, not whether it was in the future.

Investigation: Manoj found that the Zod schema validated date format but not business rules. The "no backdating" rule existed only in the Angular form's date picker configuration (minDate set to today). An API-savvy user bypassed the UI entirely with a curl command.

Fix: (1) Added a Zod .refine() that rejects dates in the past. (2) Separated validation into schema (format) and business (logic) layers — both run server-side. (3) Added a validation test suite that sends requests directly to the API (not through the Angular app) to verify all rules are enforced server-side. (4) Created a validation error response format that returns structured errors (field + message) for client-side display.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Client-side only validation → bypass via APIAlways validate server-side; client validation is UX only
Error messages expose internal schemaReturn field + generic message; log detailed errors server-side
Validation rules differ between client and serverShared Zod schemas via package; or generate client types from server schema
Complex business validation is slowRun schema validation first (fast); only run business rules if schema passes

Validation Strategy

  • Bypass test: submit invalid data directly via curl (skip UI) — verify server rejects
  • Injection test: submit MongoDB operators ($gt, $ne) in string fields — verify sanitized
  • Error format test: verify error responses don't leak schema details
  • Performance: validate 1000 requests/second, verify validation adds < 5ms per request

Definition

Architecting Input Validation for 1M+ users requires a validation system that handles high throughput without becoming a bottleneck, provides consistent validation across all entry points (API, WebSocket, background jobs), supports schema evolution without breaking existing clients, and efficiently validates complex business rules that require database lookups.

Concrete Use-Case

In a scaled ContactsHub serving 1M users, Manoj's bulk CSV import endpoint receives files with 500 rows × 15 fields each. Validation must check: (1) each field's type/format, (2) phone number format per country, (3) email uniqueness across 50M existing contacts, (4) image URL validity for Cloudinary uploads, (5) no duplicate rows within the same CSV. At peak: 100 concurrent CSV imports = 50K rows to validate simultaneously.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

import { z } from 'zod';

// Compiled phone regex per country (cached at startup)
const PHONE_PATTERNS: Record = {
  IN: /^\+91[6-9]\d{9}$/,
  US: /^\+1[2-9]\d{9}$/,
  UK: /^\+44[1-9]\d{9,10}$/,
};

const ContactRowSchema = z.object({
  name: z.string().min(1).max(200).trim(),
  email: z.string().email().toLowerCase(),
  phone: z.string().optional(),
  company: z.string().max(200).optional(),
  notes: z.string().max(2000).optional(),
});

// Bulk validation pipeline
async function validateCSVBatch(rows: any[], userId: string): Promise<{
  valid: any[]; errors: { row: number; field: string; message: string }[];
}> {
  const errors: { row: number; field: string; message: string }[] = [];
  const validRows: any[] = [];

  // Step 1: Schema validation (sync, fast)
  const schemaValid = rows.map((row, index) => {
    const result = ContactRowSchema.safeParse(row);
    if (!result.success) {
      result.error.issues.forEach(issue => {
        errors.push({ row: index + 1, field: issue.path.join('.'), message: issue.message });
      });
      return null;
    }
    return { ...result.data, _rowIndex: index };
  }).filter(Boolean);

  // Step 2: Phone format validation (sync, fast)
  schemaValid.forEach(row => {
    if (row.phone) {
      const countryCode = row.phone.startsWith('+91') ? 'IN' : row.phone.startsWith('+1') ? 'US' : 'UK';
      if (!PHONE_PATTERNS[countryCode]?.test(row.phone)) {
        errors.push({ row: row._rowIndex + 1, field: 'phone', message: `Invalid phone format for ${countryCode}` });
      }
    }
  });

  // Step 3: Email uniqueness — batch query
  const emails = schemaValid.map(r => r.email);
  const existingEmails = await db.collection('contacts').distinct('email', {
    email: { $in: emails },
    userId,
  });
  const existingSet = new Set(existingEmails);

  // Also check for duplicates within the CSV itself
  const csvEmailCount = new Map();
  schemaValid.forEach(row => {
    const indices = csvEmailCount.get(row.email) || [];
    indices.push(row._rowIndex);
    csvEmailCount.set(row.email, indices);
  });

  schemaValid.forEach(row => {
    if (existingSet.has(row.email)) {
      errors.push({ row: row._rowIndex + 1, field: 'email', message: 'Email already exists in contacts' });
    }
    const csvDupes = csvEmailCount.get(row.email);
    if (csvDupes && csvDupes.length > 1 && csvDupes[0] !== row._rowIndex) {
      errors.push({ row: row._rowIndex + 1, field: 'email', message: 'Duplicate email within CSV' });
    }
  });

  // Collect valid rows (no errors)
  const errorRowIndices = new Set(errors.map(e => e.row - 1));
  validRows.push(...schemaValid.filter(r => !errorRowIndices.has(r._rowIndex)));

  return { valid: validRows, errors };
}

// CSV import endpoint with streaming validation
app.post('/api/contacts/import', async (req, reply) => {
  const file = req.body.file;
  const rows = parseCSV(file); // Returns array of objects

  if (rows.length > 500) {
    return reply.code(400).send({ error: 'Maximum 500 rows per import' });
  }

  const { valid, errors } = await validateCSVBatch(rows, req.user.id);

  if (valid.length > 0) {
    await db.collection('contacts').insertMany(
      valid.map(r => ({ ...r, userId: req.user.id, createdAt: new Date() }))
    );
  }

  return {
    imported: valid.length,
    failed: errors.length,
    errors: errors.slice(0, 100), // Cap error list for large batches
  };
});

Debugging Narrative

Situation: ContactsHub's CSV import was taking 45 seconds for a 500-row file, making users think the upload had failed. Some users clicked "import" multiple times, creating duplicate contacts.

Investigation: Manoj profiled the import endpoint. The email uniqueness check was running 500 individual findOne queries (one per row). Each query took 80ms with MongoDB index lookup. Total: 500 × 80ms = 40 seconds just for uniqueness validation.

Fix: (1) Replaced 500 individual queries with a single distinct('email', { email: { $in: [...500 emails] } }) query — 80ms total. (2) Added in-CSV duplicate detection using a Map. (3) Added an idempotency key to prevent double-submit: the client sends a UUID with each import, server rejects if the same UUID was seen in the last hour. (4) Added streaming response: return progress events via Server-Sent Events during import. Result: 45s → 2s.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
N+1 validation queries for batch operationsBatch queries: single $in query for all items
Failing on first error loses valid rowsValidate all rows, return all errors + import valid rows
Double-submit creates duplicatesIdempotency key (UUID) per import request
Schema validation CPU overhead at high throughputPre-compiled Zod schemas; cache regex patterns at startup

Validation Strategy

  • Batch performance: validate 500 rows in < 500ms including uniqueness check
  • Error completeness: submit CSV with errors in rows 1, 50, 500 — verify all 3 errors returned
  • Idempotency: submit same CSV twice with same UUID, verify second import returns 0 imported
  • Injection test: embed MongoDB operators in CSV fields, verify sanitized

Definition

Architecting Error Handling for 1M+ users requires a centralized, structured error handling system that provides clear error responses to clients, detailed diagnostic information for developers, automated alerting for critical errors, and graceful degradation when downstream services fail. At scale, error handling must not become a performance bottleneck and must handle error storms gracefully.

Concrete Use-Case

In a scaled HR Leave Management System, Manoj designed a layered error handling strategy: (1) Domain errors (InsufficientBalance, LeaveOverlap) return 422 with business context, (2) Infrastructure errors (DB timeout, Redis down) return 503 with retry-after header, (3) Unexpected errors return 500 with correlation ID. All errors are structured JSON with consistent shape. During month-end spikes, the system handles 500 errors/minute without the error logging pipeline becoming a bottleneck.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Custom error hierarchy
class AppError extends Error {
  constructor(
    public statusCode: number,
    public code: string,
    message: string,
    public details?: any,
    public retryable: boolean = false,
  ) {
    super(message);
    this.name = 'AppError';
  }
}

class DomainError extends AppError {
  constructor(code: string, message: string, details?: any) {
    super(422, code, message, details, false);
  }
}

class InfrastructureError extends AppError {
  constructor(message: string, public retryAfterSeconds: number = 30) {
    super(503, 'SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE', message, undefined, true);
  }
}

// Centralized error handler
app.setErrorHandler(async (error, req, reply) => {
  const correlationId = req.correlationId || crypto.randomUUID();

  if (error instanceof AppError) {
    // Known application error — structured response
    if (error.statusCode >= 500) {
      req.log.error({ correlationId, error: error.message, code: error.code, stack: error.stack });
      errorMetrics.increment(error.code);
    } else {
      req.log.warn({ correlationId, code: error.code, message: error.message });
    }

    const response: any = {
      error: {
        code: error.code,
        message: error.message,
        correlationId,
        retryable: error.retryable,
      },
    };

    if (error.details) response.error.details = error.details;
    if (error instanceof InfrastructureError) {
      reply.header('Retry-After', error.retryAfterSeconds);
    }

    return reply.code(error.statusCode).send(response);
  }

  // Unexpected error — log everything, return minimal info
  req.log.error({
    correlationId,
    error: error.message,
    stack: error.stack,
    path: req.url,
    method: req.method,
    userId: req.user?.id,
  });

  errorMetrics.increment('UNEXPECTED_ERROR');

  reply.code(500).send({
    error: {
      code: 'INTERNAL_ERROR',
      message: 'An unexpected error occurred',
      correlationId,
      retryable: false,
    },
  });
});

// Error storm detection
const errorCounts = new Map();

function checkErrorStorm(errorCode: string): boolean {
  const now = Date.now();
  const entry = errorCounts.get(errorCode) || { count: 0, lastReset: now };

  if (now - entry.lastReset > 60000) {
    entry.count = 0;
    entry.lastReset = now;
  }

  entry.count++;
  errorCounts.set(errorCode, entry);

  if (entry.count > 1000) {
    // Circuit breaker: too many errors of same type
    return true;
  }
  return false;
}

// Usage in service layer
async function submitLeave(data: CreateLeaveInput, userId: string) {
  const balance = await prisma.leaveBalance.findUnique({ where: { employeeId: userId } });
  if (!balance || balance.remaining < data.days) {
    throw new DomainError('INSUFFICIENT_BALANCE',
      `You have ${balance?.remaining ?? 0} days remaining but requested ${data.days} days`,
      { remaining: balance?.remaining ?? 0, requested: data.days }
    );
  }

  try {
    return await prisma.leave.create({ data: { ...data, employeeId: userId } });
  } catch (err) {
    if (err.code === 'P2002') { // Prisma unique constraint
      throw new DomainError('LEAVE_OVERLAP', 'A leave request already exists for these dates');
    }
    throw new InfrastructureError('Database operation failed', 30);
  }
}

Debugging Narrative

Situation: During a PostgreSQL connection pool issue, the HR system logged 50K error messages in 2 minutes, filling the log storage quota and causing the logging pipeline (Fluentd) to drop subsequent logs — including logs from the recovery, making it impossible to trace the fix.

Investigation: Every failed database query generated a full stack trace log entry (2KB each). 50K entries × 2KB = 100MB of logs in 2 minutes. The Fluentd buffer overflowed, and all subsequent logs (including the successful recovery) were dropped.

Fix: (1) Implemented error storm detection: if the same error code exceeds 100 occurrences in 1 minute, aggregate into a single log entry with count. (2) Added a circuit breaker that returns 503 immediately (without hitting the DB) when error rate exceeds threshold. (3) Set log level to error for infrastructure errors (skip stack trace for repeated occurrences). (4) Added a dedicated error metrics counter (Prometheus) separate from logs — metrics survive even when logs are dropped.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Error storm fills log storageAggregate repeated errors; log count instead of individual entries
Unexpected errors expose internal detailsReturn only correlation ID; log details server-side
Retry-on-error amplifies load during outage503 with Retry-After header; client-side exponential backoff
Error handling itself throws (catch block bug)Process-level uncaughtException handler as last resort

Validation Strategy

  • Error shape test: trigger each error type, verify response matches expected JSON structure
  • Storm test: generate 1000 errors/second, verify logs contain aggregated entries (not 1000 individual)
  • Correlation ID: trigger error, verify same correlation ID appears in response and server log
  • Circuit breaker: simulate DB down, verify 503 returned within 1ms (no DB connection attempt)

Definition

Error Handling performance bottlenecks emerge when error processing itself becomes expensive: synchronous log writes blocking the event loop, stack trace generation consuming CPU, error serialization overhead, and error reporting services (Sentry, Datadog) adding network latency to error paths. At 1M users, even a 1% error rate means 10K errors per minute that must be handled efficiently.

Concrete Use-Case

In the CRM Sales Dashboard, Manoj discovered that during a MongoDB connection issue, the error handler was the bottleneck — not the failed queries. Each error triggered: (1) full stack trace capture (5ms), (2) Sentry SDK report (50ms network call), (3) structured log write with request context (2ms), and (4) Prometheus counter increment. At 500 errors/second, the error handler consumed 28.5 seconds of CPU per second — more than the event loop could handle.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Optimized error handler — non-blocking, batched
const errorBuffer: any[] = [];
let flushTimer: NodeJS.Timeout | null = null;

app.setErrorHandler(async (error, req, reply) => {
  const correlationId = req.correlationId || crypto.randomUUID();

  // Fast path: known domain errors — no stack trace, no external reporting
  if (error instanceof DomainError) {
    // Metrics only (0.1ms)
    errorMetrics.increment(error.code);
    return reply.code(error.statusCode).send({
      error: { code: error.code, message: error.message, correlationId },
    });
  }

  // Slow path: unexpected errors — but still non-blocking
  // 1. Metrics (sync, fast)
  errorMetrics.increment('unexpected_error');

  // 2. Buffer for batch processing (non-blocking)
  errorBuffer.push({
    correlationId,
    error: error.message,
    stack: error.stack, // Capture stack but don't process synchronously
    path: req.url,
    method: req.method,
    userId: req.user?.id,
    timestamp: Date.now(),
  });

  // Flush buffer every 5 seconds (batch Sentry + log writes)
  if (!flushTimer) {
    flushTimer = setTimeout(async () => {
      const batch = errorBuffer.splice(0, errorBuffer.length);
      flushTimer = null;

      if (batch.length === 0) return;

      // Batch log write
      for (const entry of batch) {
        req.log.error(entry);
      }

      // Sample for Sentry (report 10% of unexpected errors)
      const sampled = batch.filter(() => Math.random() < 0.1);
      for (const entry of sampled) {
        Sentry.captureException(new Error(entry.error), {
          extra: { correlationId: entry.correlationId, path: entry.path },
        });
      }
    }, 5000);
  }

  // Return immediately (don't wait for logging/reporting)
  reply.code(500).send({
    error: { code: 'INTERNAL_ERROR', message: 'An unexpected error occurred', correlationId },
  });
});

// Graceful shutdown: flush error buffer
process.on('SIGTERM', async () => {
  if (errorBuffer.length > 0) {
    const batch = errorBuffer.splice(0, errorBuffer.length);
    for (const entry of batch) {
      console.error(JSON.stringify(entry));
    }
  }
});

Debugging Narrative

Situation: During a MongoDB connection issue (100 errors/second), the CRM API response time for ALL requests (including successful ones) jumped from 50ms to 2 seconds. The error handler was blocking the event loop.

Investigation: Manoj added event loop delay monitoring. During the error spike, event loop delay reached 500ms. The Sentry SDK's captureException was making a synchronous HTTP call for each error (50ms each). At 100 errors/second: 5 seconds of blocking per second — impossible for a single-threaded Node.js process.

Fix: (1) Made Sentry calls fully async with batch transport (buffer errors, send every 5 seconds). (2) Added error sampling: only report 10% of unexpected errors to Sentry. (3) For known error types (domain errors), skip Sentry entirely — only increment metrics. (4) Moved log writes to async (Pino with async destination). Result: error handling overhead from 57ms to 0.6ms per error. Event loop delay stayed under 10ms even at 500 errors/second.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Error reporting blocks event loopAsync/batched Sentry transport; fire-and-forget logging
Stack trace generation is CPU-expensiveSkip stack traces for known domain error types
Error handler floods external servicesSample unexpected errors (10%); aggregate known errors by count
Error buffer lost on crashSIGTERM handler flushes buffer; write to stderr as last resort

Validation Strategy

  • Event loop test: generate 500 errors/second for 60 seconds, verify event loop delay < 50ms
  • Sentry sampling: generate 1000 errors, verify ~100 reported to Sentry (10% sample)
  • Response time: during error storm, verify successful requests still respond in < 100ms
  • Buffer flush: kill process with SIGTERM during error storm, verify buffered errors written to stderr
